metadata.description: Chinese vocabulary for family members and occupations

content.name: family_home
content.side_e: Nǐ jiā yǒu jǐ kǒu rén?
content.side_f: How many people are in your family?
content.side_g: 家 means both 'family' and 'home'; 回家 = go home, 家人 = family members. The set question 你家有几口人？uses 口 as the measure word for family members.

content.name: how_many
content.side_f: How many older brothers do you have?
content.side_g: 几 asks about small numbers (usually under 10) and requires a measure word: 几个, 几口. For larger or unknown quantities use 多少 instead.

content.name: measure_word_family
content.side_a: (measure word for number of family members)
content.side_e: Wǒ jiā yǒu wǔ kǒu rén.
content.side_f: There are five people in my family.
content.side_g: 口 is used almost exclusively for counting family members; for people in general use 个 (几个人). Literal meaning is 'mouth', reflecting the old sense of mouths to feed.

content.name: older_brother
content.side_f: My older brother is a lawyer.
content.side_g: The second 哥 is neutral tone: gē-ge. 大哥 = eldest brother, 二哥 = second older brother. In kinship terms 我哥哥 often drops the 的.

content.name: two_couple
content.side_g: Use 两 (not 二) before measure words for quantities of two: 两个人, 两口人. 二 is used for ordinals, phone numbers, and in compounds like 二姐.

content.name: younger_sister
content.side_g: Second 妹 is neutral tone: mèi-mei. 大妹妹 = older of one's younger sisters, 小妹妹 = youngest sister, a distinction used in the chapter dialogue.

content.name: and
content.side_f: I have an eldest sister and a second sister.
content.side_g: 和 (hé) connects nouns, not clauses — don't use it to mean 'and then' between sentences. In mainland speech 和 is usually hé; Taiwan often pronounces it hàn.

content.name: eldest_sister
content.side_f: My eldest sister is a lawyer.
content.side_g: 大 + kinship term marks the eldest: 大姐, 大哥. 大姐 can also be a polite address for an older woman roughly one's own age.

content.name: second_oldest_sister
content.side_f: My second-oldest sister is a doctor.
content.side_g: Ordinal 二 + kinship term ranks siblings: 二姐, 二哥, 三姐. Unlike quantity 两, ordinals always use 二.

content.name: to_do
content.side_g: 做 covers 'do/make/become' — 做工作 (do a job), 做饭 (cook), 做老师 (be/become a teacher). 做什么工作？is the standard way to ask about someone's occupation.

content.name: job_work
content.side_g: 工作 is both noun ('job') and verb ('to work'): 他在纽约工作 = he works in New York. As a noun it takes 个: 一个工作.

content.name: lawyer
content.side_f: My older brother is also a lawyer.
content.side_g: Occupations follow 是 directly with no article: 是律师, not 是一个律师 (though 一个 is possible for emphasis). 律 = law, 师 = master/professional, a suffix shared with 老师.

content.name: english_language
content.side_g: 英文 emphasizes the written/literary language, while 英语 leans spoken; in practice they're largely interchangeable. Same pattern: 中文, 法文, 日文.

content.name: both_all
content.side_g: 都 must follow the subject it quantifies and precede the verb: 我们都是学生 (✓), not *都我们是. With negation, 都不 = none, 不都 = not all.

content.name: college_student
content.side_g: Literally 大学 + 生 ('university student'). Contrast with 中学生 (middle/high schooler) and 小学生 (elementary student); 研究生 = graduate student.

content.name: university_college
content.side_g: 上大学 = attend college (enrolled); 去大学 would just mean physically going there. 在 + place + 上大学 is the set pattern heard in the dialogue.

content.name: doctor_physician
content.side_f: My second-oldest sister is a doctor.
content.side_g: 医生 is the everyday word; 大夫 (dàifu) is a colloquial synonym, and 医师 is more formal/written. 看医生 = see a doctor.
